title-group.article-title: Pecking block use at individual level is associated with improved eggshell quality and keel fractures in laying hens

contrib-group.aff: aDepartment of Animal Biosciences, Ontario Agricultural College, University of Guelph, Guelph, Ontario, N1G2W1 Canada
bCampbell Centre for the Study of Animal Welfare, University of Guelph, Guelph, Ontario N1G2W1 Canada

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Inc. on behalf of Poultry Science Association Inc.

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/).

abstract.p: Laying hens possess a calcium-specific appetite that intensifies towards lights out to meet the high demands for eggshell formation and skeletal maintenance. Pecking blocks (PBs) are edible enrichments that can serve as an additional calcium source. We explored the relationships between PB preference (PBp), PB use, keel fracture status (KS), and eggshell quality. One hundred and twenty Dekalb White and 120 Bovans Brown were housed in twelve enriched cages. Three types of PB differing in calcium and grain content were offered in a series of paired preference tests over three 4-week periods in phase 1 of the study (26 to 39 WOA). This was repeated in phase 2 (41 to 48 WOA), with three 2-week periods. Block pecking behavior was measured at the individual level by live observations of 7 focal birds per cage (n = 77) once weekly (3-time intervals). Relative PBp was based on the frequency of pecks calculated as preference ratio between PBs. Hens were categorized as PB “users” or “nonusers” based on the number of pecks at all blocks throughout the experiment. After each period, hens with PBp were isolated for egg collection. The egg weight (EW in g), shell breaking strength (SBS in kg force), and shell thickness (ST in mm) were analyzed. Using radiography, we scored keel fractures at 40 WOA (baseline) and after each period in phase 2. Consistency of hen’s PBp between phases was determined using kappa analysis and indicated moderate agreement (kappa = 0.6). PBp had no association with KS (p = 0.36), EW(p = 0.64), and SBS (p = 0.68). However, block “users” maintained ST over time (p = 0.002) and had higher SBS (p = 0.009) compared to “nonusers.” Hens who experienced a new keel fracture during phase 2 (n = 11) increased pecking in phase 2 compared to phase 1 (p= 0.023) and pecked more than hens without a keel fracture in phase 2 (n = 11; p= 0.006). This study suggests that individual hens’ use of pecking blocks may reflect individual calcium demand for eggshell formation and bone repair.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Egg-laying birds experience a higher demand for calcium compared to other vertebrates (Graveland and Berends, 1997). To meet the demands for high egg production, modern laying hens must consume about 3-4 g of calcium per day (Castillo et al., 2004; Narvaez-Solarte, et al., 2006), and therefore, commercial diets are formulated with various sources of calcium such as limestone, oyster shells (Lichovnikova, 2007), snail shells (Houndonougbo et al., 2012), and bone meal (Catli et al., 2012). When provided a free source of calcium, many hens demonstrate a specific appetite for calcium that is highly influenced by the stage of egg formation (that is, time of day) (Mongin and Sauveur, 1974). Eggshell formation occurs in the shell gland at night, and laying hens in a controlled environment increase their supplemental calcium intake between 16:00 and 20:00 h (Mongin and Sauveur, 1974). Hence, when given a choice, laying hens will adjust calcium intake to meet their individual needs for eggshell formation, which is time specific.

The healing and calcification of bone fractures is another potential source of increased calcium demand in laying hens (Whitehead and Fleming, 2000). High-producing laying hens often experience osteoporosis and bone fractures (Whitehead and Fleming, 2000), especially of the keel (Rufener and Makagon, 2020), with the highest prevalence observed mid to late lay (Eusemann et al., 2018; Petrik et al., 2015; Rufener et al., 2019). Although the cause of keel fractures is multi-factorial, nutritional factors like calcium, phosphorus, and vitamin D levels in the diet are crucial in determining hens’ bone-breaking strength (Olgun and Aygun, 2016). Hens with fresh keel fractures have been observed to have higher feed intake (Nasr et al., 2013), possibly to obtain more calcium to support healing, which involves callus formation around the fracture area within a few weeks after the injury occurs (Baur et al., 2020). Therefore, it may be beneficial to explore how the development of keel fractures in individual hens affects their calcium appetite when offered pecking blocks (PBs) (a source of calcium) and vice versa.

Wild birds with access to soil exhibit calcium-seeking behaviors like burrowing into the soil, consuming sand, and little stones during periods of egg production (Graveland and Berends, 1997). Although no longer typically done in modern commercial production systems, the provision of a supplemental calcium source might be of benefit to laying hens. Providing hens with supplemental calcium would enable hens to adjust their calcium intake by self-selection to meet their individual calcium needs without having to consume additional energy-deriving feedstuffs from the formulated complete diet (Griminger and Lutz, 1964). Pecking blocks, marketed to control feather pecking and enhance foraging behaviors in laying hens, are edible enrichments that can serve as an additional calcium source for hens.

There are many different commercially available pecking blocks (Iqbal et al., 2020) with varying nutrient composition, calcium levels, and sensory characteristics. Therefore, hens may be attracted to pecking blocks for a variety of reasons, including calcium appetite. Our previous reports indicated that brown- and white-feathered laying hens preferred pecking blocks with different nutrient contents (Ehigbor et al., 2025) and that hens of white-feathered strains pecked at calcium-based pecking blocks more in the afternoon, possibly due to the intensification of their calcium appetite toward the end of the day (Ehigbor et al., 2025; McAuley et al., 2025; Hipkin et al., 2024). We also found that block disappearance, an estimate of ingestion, could average upwards of 3 g/hen/day depending on the PB offered (Ehigbor et al., 2025; McAuley et al., 2025). However, those data were analyzed at the group level, and any differences between individual hens and whether individual hens were consistent in their pecking block preference over time were not reported.

In this study, our objective was to explore the association between relative preferences for different pecking blocks, keel bone fracture status, and eggshell quality of individual hens. First, we investigated the consistency of individual hens' pecking block preferences over time. Then, we determined the association between pecking block preference, overall pecking block use, eggshell characteristics, and keel fractures. We used pecking behavior directed at blocks as a proxy for block ingestion since we previously demonstrated a significant correlation between block pecking behavior and block disappearance by weight (McAuley et al., 2025). We hypothesized that hens who prefer mineral-based pecking blocks or hens who use blocks more overall will have better eggshell characteristics. For the relationship between keel fractures and pecking block use, we had a bidirectional hypothesis. We hypothesized that hens who prefer mineral-based blocks or who use pecking blocks more overall would have fewer keel fractures (i.e., stronger keels) and that hens with recently fractured keel bones would show higher pecking block use.

sec.title: Animals and housing
sec.p: This experiment was approved by the Animal Care Committee at the University of Guelph, Ontario, Canada (Animal Use Protocol 4749), and all individuals involved in this study were trained in poultry handling and care. The birds were housed at the Arkell Research Station at the University of Guelph, Ontario, Canada. All individuals and procedures for conducting radiographs were trained and approved by the Environmental Health and Safety (EHS) at the University of Guelph.

A total of 120 beak-treated Dekalb White and 120 beak-treated Bovans Brown were used in this study. Birds were hatched at a commercial facility (McKinley Hatchery St Mary’s, Ontario, Canada), transported to the Arkell Poultry Research Station at one day of age, and strains were raised separately but under similar environmental conditions in identical rearing aviaries. At 18 weeks of age, they were moved to 12 large, enriched cages that were modified for the experiment (Farmer Automatic, Clark AG systems, Caledonia, ON - H = 60 cm x W = 360 cm x L = 116 cm). Each test cage had two identical sides, with a 38 cm x 46 cm nest, a 43 cm x 36 cm scratch mat behind the nest, and a blue pecking block container placed on each of the scratch mats. A central perch ran across the length of the cage and acted as a visual divider separating both sides of the cage (see Ehigbor et al., 2025 for illustration). Each cage also had an internal LED lighting strip of 95.25 cm in length. There were 20 hens of the same strain in each cage, with 7 selected randomly as focal animals and given unique leg bands for identification. At 28 weeks of age, one of the cages of brown hens was removed from the trial due to a feather pecking outbreak, resulting in a total of 11 cages and 77 focal animals in the experiment.

The hens were maintained on a 14-h light period and 10-h dark period, with room lights coming on at 4:00 h and dusk starting at 18:00 h. They were fed commercial layer diet in crumbles on ad libitum basis (Floradale, Ontario, Canada). The chemical composition of the diet is shown in Table 1. Before the start of our experiment, birds were habituated to the test cage for 8 weeks and to human presence by the investigators sitting in front of cages for extended periods to prepare for live behavior observations. Habituation was done to avoid hens being distracted by human presence during data collection.Table 1
Calculated chemical composition of the diet fed to the birds.Table 1
NutrientAmount
Crude protein (min)18.0 %
Crude fat (min)5.5 %
Crude fibre (max)2.0 %
Calcium (actual)4.24 %
Phosphorus (actual)0.68 %
Sodium (actual)0.18 %
Vitamin A (min)16,500 IU/kg
Vitamin D (min)4,130 IU/kg
Vitamin E (min)60 IU/kg

sec.title: Pecking blocks
sec.p:
Three commercially available pecking blocks were used. Ingredients and guaranteed analyses listed on the label for each block were: Pecking block A: calcium carbonate, monobasic calcium phosphate, magnesium oxide, wheat, cane molasses, sodium chloride, ground corn, and oats with guaranteed analysis of magnesium (2 %), calcium (19 %), and phosphorus (4 %). Pecking block B: calcium carbonate, cane molasses, wheat, barley, vegetable oil, corn, oyster shell, lignin sulfonate, sunflower seeds, and whole milo with guaranteed analysis of calcium (16 −19.2 %), crude protein (3 %), phosphorus (0.8 %), crude fiber (3 %), crude fat (1 %), and 6 % moisture. Pecking block C: calcium carbonate, monocalcium phosphate, salt, magnesium oxide, sodium sulfate, magnesium sulfate, wheat bran, with calcium (21 – 24 %) and other minerals. We ordered two lots of each pecking block (A, B, and C) and submitted samples of each for chemical composition analyses at a commercial laboratory (SGS Canada Inc. Lab, Agriculture and Food, Guelph, ON, Canada) (Table 2). The difference in some nutrient values between lots was due to the heterogeneity of the block samples (e.g., mixed grains). The pecking blocks were manufactured in different shapes and sizes; hence, using a band saw, we cut the blocks into similar rectangular shapes and sizes, which averaged 9.14 cm in width x 22.9 cm in length x 6.35 cm in height. The block size was chosen as we assumed it would allow several hens to access the blocks simultaneously. Blocks A and B were dark brown in color, whereas block C was light gray.Table 2
Analyzed composition of the pecking blocks (as is). Each type of block was sampled and analyzed from two lots* delivered by the pecking block companies fand used or the entire study.Table 2
SampleMoisture %Crude protein %Crude fat %Ash %CHO %Gross energy (kcal/100 g)Ca %P %Na %K %Mg %
Block A Lot 16.862.920.2661.428.56128.2415.293.481.280.531.76
Block A Lot 28.7210.593.3528.8848.46266.317.652.191.140.861.88
Block B Lot 17.263.922.1446.0440.64197.513.890.722.120.570.17
Block B Lot 27.162.991.4955.9632.4154.9918.660.852.380.50.17
Block C Lot 1Not analyzed26.365.044.94<0.032.3
Block C Lot 2Not analyzed27.234.854.28<0.032.4
*Differences between different lots of the same block may be due to the heterogeneous nature of the block samples.

sec.title: Experimental design
sec.p: There were 2 phases of data collection in this experiment. Phase 1 had three 4-week periods (26 – 39 weeks of age), and phase 2 had three 2-week periods (41 – 48 weeks of age). Each period was separated by one week of washout. There were 3 sequential paired preference tests (A v B, B v C, A v C), and 4 out of 12 cages were offered the same pairs of blocks per period in phases 1 and 2, respectively. The paired preference tests were balanced across strains. For each cage, the same pairings of blocks offered in periods 1, 2, and 3 (phase 1) were repeated in periods 4, 5, and 6, respectively.
Over the course of the two phases, behavioral data were collected and summarized at the end of each period and used to select individual hens for egg collection and keel measures.

sec.title: Data collection
sec.p: Behavioral data collection. Through live behavior observations, four observers quantified individual focal hens’ behavior (Ehigbor et al., 2025). Briefly, on one day per week all focal birds were observed continuously during 4-minute observation periods during the morning, mid-day and before lights out. Block pecking was counted each time a hen contacted the block with her beak in a downward motion and recorded using Noldus ® Observer XT version 16 Pocket Observer software (Noldus Information Technology BV, Waageningen, The Netherlands). Thus, the frequency of pecks at each block by each focal bird was measured for 12 minutes each week over the entire experiment (except during washout).

Selection of hens for egg sampling. At the end of each period, we summed the frequency of pecks each focal bird directed at the different pecking blocks across the weeks (48 minutes for each of the three periods in Phase 1 and 24 minutes for each of the periods in Phase 2). Based on this criterion, we calculated a preference-ratio to determine the relative preference of each individual hen for a specific block in the paired test for that period. For example, for the B and C pairwise comparison:PeckRatioB=PecksatB/PecksatB+C

We then used the following definitions that:
<.4 indicated a preference for the C block,
≥.4-.6 indicated no preference and
>.6 indicated a preference for the B block.
Similar preference ratios were determined for the AB and AC paired preference tests.

Using the preference ratios, birds were selected for egg sampling. During hen selection for egg sampling, we attempted to balance block preference, block use, and strain within each collection period (Table 3). Additionally, some control birds that were never observed to peck the blocks were selected.Table 3
Number of birds sampled for egg collection in each period, with their strain, block preference and pecking block use status specified.Table 3
PeriodNumber of hens for egg samplingWhite(W)Brown(B)Pref. APref. BPref. CUsersbNonusersb
WBWBWB
12414102231411311+
23015153324421812*
33017132116811911#
42817111333601612*
53219132144722012*
63418163245532212*
Total178a100781312172334910870
+ indicates 5 W and 6 B; # indicates 6 W and 5 B; *indicates equal number of W and B.a
Some birds were used in more than one period. Egg sampling was done from 67 of the 77 focal birds over all 6 periods.b
Users and nonusers were categorized from behavioral data collected during all six periods.
Hens were housed individually for 2 - 3 nights, with 2 eggs collected from each hen per collection period.
Pref. means block preference.

Egg collection and measurements. Within 2 days after the period ended, hens selected for egg sampling were moved to individual housing (L = 66.04 cm x W = 30.48 cm x H = 48.26 cm tall at the feeder) (but with visual and auditory access to other selected focal birds) for two to three nights. Afterward, they were returned to their home cage. Individual cages contained a nest, scratch mat, and perch. The birds were provided feed and water ad libitum, and their eggs were collected, labeled, and analyzed for egg weight in grams (g), shell thickness in millimeters (mm), and shell-breaking force in kilogram of force (kgf). Eggs were weighed on a digital scale (Sartorius, Mississauga, Ontario, Canada), the eggshell thickness was measured using an ultrasound eggshell thickness gauge (Orka Technology Ltd., Utah, USA), and the shell breaking strength was measured using an Orka Egg Force Reader (Orka Technology Ltd., Utah, USA).

Keel scoring. In the second phase only (which allowed for regular keel assessment), we assessed the keel bone fracture status via radiography in all focal birds from all the cages (n = 77) at week 40 (baseline) and at the end of period 4 (43 WOA), 5 (46 WOA), and 6 (49 WOA). The birds were hung from padded shackles by their feet, radiographed using a diagnostic X-ray machine (80 kV, 2.5mAs, 650 nm; model VET-20BT, POSKOM Co. Ltd., Gyeonggi-do, Korean; United Radiology Systems, Inc., Deerfield IL, USA), and then returned to their respective cages. The entire X-ray procedure took less than a minute for each bird. Digital images of the radiographs were analyzed using Metron Imaging Software (Metron-DVM 7.20, Metron Imaging, Creston CA, USA). The keel radiographs were scored by one observer on an ascending scale of 0-5 (based on the severity of keel fractures) following Rufener et al. (2018). The scale was continuous between 0 (not fractured) and 5 (severely fractured). The observer practiced with the training tool from Rufener et al. (2018) till an intra-observer reliability of 100 % was reached before scoring began. The observer was blinded to the bird's pecking block preference.

sec.title: Data processing (descriptive statistics)
sec.p: Individual variation in pecking blockuse. Individual bird variation in pecking block use was determined as the frequency of pecks that each focal bird directed towards each block over the course of the experiment (phases 1 and 2). Microsoft Excel was then used to create stacked bar charts to examine trends.

Criteria for categorizing individual hen status (pecking block users and nonusers). Although hens were selected for egg collection based on preference ratio for specific block type collected within a period, we also categorized birds as block ‘users’ and ‘non-users’ based on their behavior observed throughout the entire duration of the experiment. The total number of pecks performed at all blocks combined over the entire observation period (216 minutes per bird) were plotted for all birds sampled for egg collection (N = 67). The distribution (Fig. 1) was visually assessed for a natural breakoff point. We identified hens with over 100 pecks as “users” (N = 31) and less than 100 pecks as “nonusers” (N = 36). The median was at 75 pecks.Fig. 1
Frequency of pecks at all blocks / 216 mins of observation (phase 1 + 2) in individual hens selected for egg collection during the experiment. The red arrow shows a cut-off point in selecting users and nonusers.Fig 1

For analysis of the keel data, the sum of pecks by all focal hens (N = 77) in the experiment (phase 1 and 2) were used to categorize hens’ user status. As above, data were graphed on Microsoft Excel for visual assessment (data not shown) (Users: N = 39 hens; Nonusers: N = 38 hens). Here, the cut-off was the median, which was 102 pecks.

sec.title: Statistical analyses
sec.p: Data were analyzed using SAS software version 9.4 (SAS Institute Inc., North Carolina, USA).

The consistency of focal birds' pecking block preference over the two phases was determined by creating frequency and cross-tabulation tables (Proc FREQ). Through the tables, we determined the kappa value for the ratio of agreement between each bird’s relative preference in the first and second phases when they were provided the same paired preference tests.

Tests for relationships between eggshell quality data and pecking block preference were analyzed using a generalized linear mixed model (Proc GLIMMIX) to determine the effects of strain, period, pecking block preference, and their interactions on egg weight, shell thickness, and shell-breaking strength. The experimental unit was individual hen. The residuals were visually assessed for normality, and were normally distributed with a Shapiro-Wilk value of 0.99. The post-hoc analysis of the least square means was obtained using the Tukey method of mean comparisons. During egg collection periods, two eggs were usually collected from each individual hen, and the mean for the two days was used for the egg characteristics analysis. Although some birds were repeatedly used for egg collection in different periods, a model that accounted for repeated measures could not be fit. Therefore, egg values from the same hens collected in different periods were treated as independent of each other.

Using the same methods as above, we analyzed the differences in eggshell quality measured in block users vs. non-users. The statistical model included the egg weight, eggshell thickness, or shell-breaking strength as dependent variables, and status (user and nonuser), strain, phase, and their interactions as independent variables. Residuals were normally distributed, with a Shapiro-Wilk value of 0.99.

The keel scores were analyzed using a generalized linear mixed model (Proc GLIMMIX), with the keel score as the dependent variable and strain, period, pecking block preference, and their interactions as the independent variables. The residuals were visually assessed for normality, and the residuals were normally distributed with a Shapiro-Wilk value of 0.91. The post-hoc analysis of the least square means was obtained using the Tukey method of means comparisons.

We used this same method to analyze for any differences between the keel scores in pecking block users vs nonusers. The statistical model included keel score as the dependent variable and status (user and nonuser), strain, period, and their interactions as fixed effects. The residuals were normally distributed, and the Shapiro-Wilk value was 0.93. Data are presented as LSMean ± SE.

sec.title: Results
sec.p:
Individual variation in pecking block use. Fig. 2 shows the individual variation in pecking block use for the white and brown strains combined in phases 1 (Fig. 2a) and 2 (Fig. 2b). Individual birds varied considerably in their use of PB, ranging from 0 to 753 pecks delivered at the pecking blocks in Phase 1 (144 mins of observation) and 0 to 867 pecks in Phase 2 (72 mins of observation). The graph shows that the B and C blocks were used the most in both phases. Some hens were only observed to use a single type of block throughout this study; some used two out of three, while others used all three pecking blocks across both phases, but at different pecking frequencies.Fig. 2
Frequency of pecks by individual hen. Pecks delivered to the three different pecking blocks are illustrated by the different colors in the stacked bars. (A). Phase 1 (144mins of observation) (B). Phase 2 (72mins of observation).Fig 2

Consistency of hens' pecking block preference when offered the same pairwise comparisons in phase 1 vs phase 2. The distribution of birds with the same pecking block preference when offered the same pairwise comparisons in phases 1 and 2 is shown in Table 4. There were 49 out of 77 hens with a relative preference for a block in both phases, and most of these birds had a preference for the same block multiple times. Out of 85 possible occurrences of birds who showed a preference for a pecking block in both phases, there were 63 times when birds were consistent in their preference. For the A block, there were 12 occurrences when it was preferred in both phases, 25 for the B block, and 26 for the C block. There was a moderate agreement in pecking block preference between both phases (kappa = 0.6), indicating that overall, the birds were fairly consistent in their relative preferences for the different blocks.Table 4
Frequency of times bird demonstrated block (A, B, C) preferences when offered the same pairwise comparison in phase 1 and phase 2. Kappa = 0.6.Table 4
Phase 2Kappa = 0.6
Phase 1ABCTotal
AFrequency%1214.1255.8867.062327.06
BFrequency%33.532529.4144.713237.65
CFrequency%11.1833.532630.593035.29
TotalFrequency%1618.823338.823642.3585100.00
Bolded Values in red are the total number of times birds had the same block preference in phases 1 and 2 for that specific block.

Association between pecking block preference, block use, and eggshell characteristics. Table 5 shows the association between pecking block preference and user status on egg characteristics for all periods and both strains combined. There were no significant relationships between pecking block preference ( p = 0.64), period ( p = 0.30), strain ( p = 0.24), or any interactions ( p > 0.05) on egg weight (g), or between pecking block preference and eggshell thickness (in mm; p = 0.92). There was an effect of period on eggshell thickness (in mm; p < 0.0001), with periods 2 and 6 being higher than periods 1 and 3. Periods 4 and 5 had similar eggshell thickness but were not significantly different from periods 1, 3, and 6 (data not shown). There was no effect of strain on eggshell thickness ( p = 0.66) nor any interaction effects ( p > 0.05). Similar to shell thickness, there were no significant relationships between pecking block preference and shell-breaking strength (in kgf; p = 0.68). There was an effect of period on shell-breaking strength ( p = 0.001; data not shown). The highest shell-breaking strength was observed in the first period. However, this was not statistically different from periods 5 and 6. There was no effect of strain on the shell-breaking strength ( p = 0.71) or any interaction effects ( p > 0.05).Table 5
Effect of pecking block preference and user status on egg characteristics. Table showing the total number of hens with a preference for each block across all periods (strains combined) and the mean values and standard errors of their egg characteristics.Table 5
NEgg weight (grams)LSMeans±SEEggshell thickness (mm)LSMeans±SEEggshell breaking strength (kgForce)LSMeans±SE
Block Preference
A2462.56±0.900.38±0.0074.15±0.007
B3962.34±0.710.39±0.0054.21±0.005
C4361.57±0.720.38±0.0064.05±0.006
p-value0.640.920.68
User Status
User3161.91±0.490.39±0.0044.29±0.09a
Non-User3662.85±0.540.38±0.0053.96±0.10b
p-value0.200.350.0009a,b
Means with different superscripts are significantly different.

There was no effect of user status ( p = 0.20), strain ( p = 0.07), or phase ( p = 0.62) on egg weight (g), but there was phase by strain interaction (in g; p = 0.015). Brown hens had heavier eggs in Phase 1 (64. 13 ± 0.78) compared to white hens in Phase 1 (60. 99 ± 0.71). Although there was no effect of user status ( p = 0.35), strain ( p = 0.77), or phase ( p = 0.40) on shell thickness, there was a phase by status interaction ( p = 0.002; Fig. 3). Eggshell thickness in pecking block users remained similar between Phases 1 and 2, whereas nonusers experienced a drop in shell thickness over time. Although a significant strain by user status interaction on shell thickness was also observed ( p = 0.0148), there were no differences between pairwise comparisons from the Tukey test. User status affected shell breaking strength (Table 5). Users had a higher shell-breaking strength compared to nonusers. There was no effect of strain on shell-breaking strength (in kgf; p = 0.98), but as would be expected, shell-breaking strength decreased over time (LSMean ± SE phase 1: 4. 24 ± 0.10, phase 2: 3. 97 ± 0.09; p = 0.047).Fig. 3
Effect of phase by status interaction ( p = 0.002) on eggshell thickness (mm). a,bBars with different superscripts correspond to significant differences ( p < 0.05).Fig 3

Association between pecking block preference, block use, and keel score. There was no relationship between pecking block preference and keel score ( p = 0.36). Although numerically birds with a preference for the C block (calcium-based) had the lowest average keel score (LSMean ± SE: 0. 52 ± 0.72) compared to those who preferred A (0. 90 ± 0.90) and B (0. 80 ± 0.71). Block use (user vs nonuser) also did not affect keel fracture score ( p = 0.70), but there was an effect of strain ( p < 0.0001), with brown hens having a significantly higher mean score (0. 93 ± 0.06) compared to whites (0. 49 ± 0.06). Period affected keel fracture score ( p = 0.005), with hens having significantly lower keel fracture scores at baseline (0. 46 ± 0.08) compared to periods 5 (0. 86 ± 0.08) and 6 (0. 79 ± 0.08).

At the baseline measure (40WOA), 75 % of the hens selected for sampling had already sustained some fracture, but none was higher in severity than 1.8 out of 5. There were 20 hens out of 77 who had no fractures at baseline. Of those 20 hens, 11 sustained a new fracture in phase 2 (keel score greater than zero), while 9 never experienced a fracture. These 9 birds with no fractures had a range of 0 – 47 pecks at all blocks during phase 2. In contrast, of the 11 birds who experienced a new fracture during phase 2, two were not observed to peck any block, two had less or equal to 35 pecks, and 7 had 150 - 282 pecks delivered to blocks. As these results were unexpected, post hoc T-tests were used to analyze the mean number of pecks in phase 2 as well as changes (Δ) in block pecking behavior between phase 1 and phase 2 for hens that experienced a fracture in phase 2 (n = 11) versus those that did not (n = 9). The number of pecks directed at blocks during phase 2 by birds who sustained new fractures during phase 2 was significantly higher than those who did not (22. 0 ± 5.2 versus 3. 4 ± 1.2 pecks/observation/hen; p = 0.006). Additionally, hens that sustained a fracture in phase 2 pecked more at the blocks during phase 2 than they did in phase 1 (mean Δ = 11. 2 ± 4.1 pecks/observation/hen) whereas hens with no fracture pecked less in phase 2 compared to phase 1 (mean Δ = −7. 4 ± 6.1; p = 0.023) suggesting that hens suffering from new fractures showed an increased number of pecks at the blocks.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: Laying hens have been found to show individual differences in their supplemental calcium intake (Griminger and Lutz, 1964; Morrissey, 2017; Taher et al., 1984) and may be attracted to pecking blocks due to their calcium appetite (McAuley et al., 2025). Therefore, individual differences in pecking block use may be caused by or be a consequence of a hen’s physiological status or production traits. In this study, hens did, in fact, demonstrate a large variation in pecking block use as well as individual preferences for different pecking blocks, with preferences relatively stable over time. We hypothesized that hens who preferred the mineral-based blocks or those who used pecking block more overall would have better eggshell characteristics. We also hypothesized that there might be some relationship between pecking block use and keel status; either pecking block users may have fewer keel fractures because they supplement their calcium intake throughout lay or, on the other hand, fracture status may affect pecking block use and could be the driving force behind their appetite for these calcium-rich blocks.

In support of our first set of hypotheses, we found that hens who were pecking block users had stronger eggshells. Pecking block users had a significantly higher eggshell-breaking strength compared to nonusers. Furthermore, hens who are pecking block users maintained a higher eggshell thickness over time than nonusers. A laying hen's eggshell is comprised of organic components and minerals (calcium carbonate, phosphorus, etc.), which play a key role in the eggshell integrity and dietary sources are critical (Dennis et al., 1996; Roberts, 2004). Although we also hypothesized that hens who preferred the mineral-based block (C) would have stronger eggshells, all of the pecking blocks used in this study contained varying concentrations of calcium, and hens could derive calcium from either of these blocks regardless of which block they preferred. Our results corroborate findings that increased pecking block ingestion improves eggshell quality (McAuley et al., 2025). Pecking blocks, a supplemental calcium source, possibly provided the necessary nutrients our laying hens needed to improve their eggshell quality at the phase of life when eggshell quality usually declines (as seen in nonusers). Therefore, our evidence suggests that pecking block users derived the necessary nutrients to maintain eggshell strength as they aged. However, because pecking blocks were not available to the hens during the one to two days of egg collection, it might also be argued that hens who produce eggs with better eggshell characteristics have a higher motivation to use and ingest pecking blocks. Future studies may explore the exact mechanisms of eggshell differences between pecking block users and nonusers.

Although there was no relationship between pecking block preference or overall use and keel fracture status, the occurrence of new keel fractures in periods of phase 2 in our study was generally associated with increased pecking directed at the pecking blocks. This showed that laying hens with new keel fractures were observed to peck more at the pecking blocks during phase 2 than hens who did not have new fractures. This provides some support for our hypothesis that physiological status (fractured keels) may influence calcium appetite and the use of pecking blocks. In humans, nutritional interventions like supplementing calcium and vitamin D improved recovery from hip fractures and helped prevent further fractures (Cianferotti et al., 2024; Fischer et al., 2018). Similar results have been found in rats (Yeh et al., 2024). Our findings emphasize the importance of pecking blocks for laying hens and how they provide the opportunity to self-select nutrients. It is important to note that the keel scores in our study were relatively low, indicating only single or minor fractures, and the overall mean score was less than 1 out of 5. Keel status was only assessed in phase 2 because the fracture prevalence previously reported for hens housed in the same cages as in the current study increased after 35 weeks of age and older (Casey-Trott et al., 2017). However, phase 2 only captured 41 to 49 weeks of age. Future studies should investigate the relationship between pecking block use and keel fracture in hens throughout their laying phase.

sec.title: Limitations
sec.p: In this study, we used pecking behavior to estimate ingestion of pecking blocks because we previously showed that pecking behavior and block disappearance by weight were significantly correlated (Ehigbor et al., 2025; McAuley et al., 2025). However, the correlation between behavior and block disappearance was higher when using video scan samples compared to live observations, since data could be collected more often across the day (Ehigbor et al., 2025). Use of live observations allowed for more detailed behavior of individual hens, but only captured 12 minutes per individual hen per week. Live observations were necessary to obtain “real time” data that would help in identifying hens for egg collection. Future work should take more sensitive measures of pecking block use and ingestion (e.g., video observations or measures of block ingestion for individual hens).

Also, hens in our study did not have pecking blocks during the night preceding laying of the eggs we measured. The absence of blocks immediately prior to shell formation could have minimized the effects of the pecking blocks on the eggshell characteristics, since nutrients from blocks were not immediately available. Future work should employ experimental methods that allow for egg collection from laying hens that have continuous access to pecking blocks.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: This study showed that individual hens have distinct choices and variations regarding pecking block preference, which are likely to stay the same over time. Preference for mineral- versus grain-based pecking blocks was not linked to eggshell quality or keel bone fracture. However, laying hens of both white and brown strains who were observed to use pecking blocks more, maintained a higher eggshell thickness over time and had stronger eggshells than hens who were not observed to peck blocks very often or at all. There was some evidence that hens experiencing new keel fractures pecked at blocks more than those without fractures. This study suggests that individual hens’ use of pecking blocks may reflect individual calcium demand for eggshell formation and bone repair.

sec.title: Funding
sec.p: This research was funded by Egg Farmers of Canada and the Ontario Agri-Food Innovation Alliance Fund, grant number 05531. NSERC (Natural Sceinces and Engineering Council of Canada) to the funding agencies

sec.title: Declaration of generative AI and AI-assisted technologies in the writing process
sec.p: During the preparation of this work, the author(s) used Grammarly in order to check grammar and punctuation. After using this tool, the author(s) reviewed and edited the content as needed and took full responsibility for the content of the publication.

sec.title: CRediT authorship contribution statement
sec.p: Tunmise F. Ehigbor: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. A. Michelle Edwards: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Ana K. Rentsch: Formal analysis, Methodology. Elijah G. Kiarie: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Alexandra Harlander: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Tina M. Widowski: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

sec.title: Disclosures
sec.p: We declare no conflicts of interest. The funders and pecking block companies had no role in the study's design, data collection, analysis, interpretation, manuscript writing, or decision to publish the results. They were contacted to review our description of the pecking blocks in this manuscript for publishing.

T.M.W. holds the Egg Farmers of Canada Chair in Poultry Welfare. T.M.W. reports that Egg Farmers of Canada provided financial support. T.M.W. reports a relationship with Egg Farmers of Canada that includes consulting or advisory services.

ack.title: Acknowledgments
ack.p: Thanks to the staff at Arkell Research Station, all research assistants who participated in data collection, and my birds for participating in this excellent research. We appreciate Noldus (www.noldus.com) and their support staff for helping with the pocket observer software. Many thanks to Ana Rentsch, Ph.D. (University of Guelph, Ontario), for scoring the keels of our hens.
